mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW M3 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned BMW M3 engine typically costs £4,500–£10,000+ supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the S54 at £5,000–£8,000 to the S65, S55 and S58 at £6,000–£10,000+. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW M3 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S58B30 in the G80 — widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern performance engine. The S54B32 in the E46 is a masterpiece once the VANOS and rod bearings have been addressed. The S65B40 in the E90 is a masterpiece once the rod bearings have been addressed.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW M3 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but an S65 (E90) without documented rod bearing replacement or an S55 (F80) without a crank hub fix are the two purchases we consistently advise against. The engines themselves are masterpieces; the known risks are the problem. An S58 (G80) has no such caveats.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my M3 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: By engine: rod bearing knock (S65/S54), crank hub spin (S55/S58), VANOS rattle (S54), timing chain rattle (S14). On the S65 and S54, cold-start knock or metallic noise at high RPM is a warning. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £4,500–£10,000+ engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW M3 engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the M3's values are strong enough to justify engine work, especially on the E46, E90, F80 and G80. Against a £40,000 F80, a £7,000–£10,000+ S55 recon is 18–25% — ✅ safe. Against a £60,000 G80, a £7,000–£10,000+ S58 recon is 12–17% — ✅ safe. Against a £15,000 E46, a £5,000–£8,000 S54 recon is 33–53% — ⚠️ watch. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW M3 should I buy?

acceptedAnswer.text: F80 M3 Competition with the S55 — 444 bhp, proven engine, and depreciated into sensible territory (£30,000–£40,000). Budget for a crank hub fix (£1,500–£3,000) or buy one that's already been done. For the purist, E46 M3 with the S54 — the greatest M3 ever made, but budget for VANOS (£1,500–£3,000) and rod bearings (£2,500–£4,000). For the future, G80 with the S58 — the most reliable M3 ever made. For the V8 experience, E90 with the S65 — the only V8 M3, but budget for rod bearings (£2,500–£4,000) before you buy.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW M3 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (E30, E36, E46, E90, F80, G80) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: BMW M3 vs M4 — which is more reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Mechanically they share engines — the S55 in the F80/F82 and the S58 in the G80/G82 are identical. The M3 has four doors; the M4 has two. Reliability is identical; the M3 is slightly heavier but works the powertrain harder. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The M3 offers more practicality; the M4 offers more style.

hero.h1: M3 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every BMW M3 engine across six generations — from the S14 four-cylinder of the E30 to the S58 straight-six of the G80. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's most iconic M car. Backed by real UK M3 engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The M3 rule — pay to play: an S55/S58 recon at £7,000–£10,000+ against a £35,000–£60,000 car is 12–29% — ✅ safe on most examples. On a high-mileage E90 S65 at £12,000, a £6,000–£10,000+ recon is 50–83% — ⚠️ watch. On an E46 S54 at £15,000, a £5,000–£8,000 recon is 33–53% — ⚠️ watch. The M3's values are generally strong enough to justify engine work, but know what you're getting into.
★★★★☆ — the S54 and S58 are amongst BMW's finest engines; the S65 is a masterpiece with rod bearing caveats; the S55 is proven but the crank hub is the Achilles heel
S58B30 (G80) — widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern performance engine
S65B40 (E90) — rod bearing failure • S55B30 (F80) — crank hub spin • S54B32 (E46) — VANOS and rod bearings
F80 M3 Competition with S55 — 444 bhp, proven engine, depreciated, and the sweet spot in the range
G80 M3 with S58 — 473–503 bhp, the most reliable M engine, and available with a manual
S65 rod bearings / S55 crank hub — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
👑 The Original M Car — the car that defined the high-performance saloon segment

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The M3 is BMW's performance icon — five generations of engineering excellence and driver engagement. The S54 and S58 are amongst the finest engines ever made; the S65's rod bearings and the S55's crank hub are the only clouds. Buy the newest S58 you can afford, or hunt for a well-maintained F80 Competition — and on any E90 S65, budget for rod bearings before you buy.

H2: BMW M3 Generations — Find Your Vehicle

rows.metric: Sub-headline: Six generations, 40 years, and one nameplate that defined the high-performance saloon. The right M3 depends on your budget, your engine preference, and your tolerance for maintenance.

rows.value: Our Verdict: The original M3 — the car that started it all. The S14 four-cylinder is a masterpiece of 1980s engineering, revving to 7,500 rpm. Now a six-figure classic where engine work is restoration, not repair. The E30 M3 is the ultimate M3 investment.

Our Verdict: The first M3 with a six-cylinder engine — and a genuine driver's car. The Euro-spec S50 with individual throttle bodies is the one to buy; the US-spec S52 is good but less special. Cooling systems and rear subframe mounts are the caveats.

rows.metric: BMW M3 E46 🔥 Most Enquired — classic era [BMW-VERIFIED]

rows.value: Our Verdict: The M3 at its peak — often called the greatest M3 ever made. The S54 is one of BMW's finest engines, revving to 8,000 rpm with individual throttle bodies. VANOS and rod bearings are the defining caveats; budget for preventative work. A sorted E46 is a six-figure classic.

rows.metric: BMW M3 E90/E92/E93 🔥 Most Enquired — S65 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]

rows.value: Our Verdict: The only V8 M3 — the S65 is a masterpiece that revs to 8,400 rpm. The rod bearing failure is the defining risk: budget £2,500–£4,000 for preventative replacement before they fail. The E90 saloon is the rarest; the E92 coupé is the most desirable.

rows.metric: BMW M3 F80 🔥 Most Enquired — 405–444 bhp [BMW-VERIFIED]

rows.value: Our Verdict: The first turbocharged M3 — and the generation that divides opinion. The S55 is proven and powerful, but the crank hub is the Achilles heel. The Competition (444 bhp) is the sweet spot; the CS (454 bhp) is the ultimate F80. Budget for a crank hub fix (£1,500–£3,000) or buy one that's already been done.

Our Verdict: The sixth-generation M3 — the most powerful and the most reliable. The S58 is widely regarded as BMW's most dependable modern performance engine. Available with a six-speed manual (473 bhp) or automatic (503 bhp). The G80 is the M3 for the modern era — buy it with confidence.
The Engine Choice — The Line That Matters Most

engineDatabase.h2: BMW M3 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine ever fitted to a BMW M3 — verified codes, specifications and real UK ownership data.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: S54, S65, S55 and S58 ratings: 90% confidence — these engines are well-documented across the BMW M range with clear patterns from specialist rebuilders and enthusiast communities. S14, S50 and S52 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — decades of documented patterns from the E30/E36 era, but not captured in proprietary volume data. Data note (internal) — corrections applied: Correction applied: The S65 V8 (E90) rod bearing failure is the defining issue of the generation — bearings fail catastrophically, taking the crankshaft with them. Preventative replacement is recommended at 60,000–80,000 miles. Correction applied: The S55 (F80) crank hub is not keyed to the crankshaft — it relies on friction alone. Under high load (especially on tuned cars), the hub can spin, causing piston-to-valve contact. This is the defining risk of the F80 generation. Correction applied: The S58 (G80) has a revised crank hub design that addresses the weakness of the S55. The S58 is widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern performance engine. Correction applied: The S54 (E46) has two defining risks — VANOS failure and rod bearing wear. Both are well-documented and repairable. A sorted E46 with these addressed is one of BMW's most reliable engines. Correction applied: The E30 M3's S14 is a four-cylinder, not a six-cylinder. This was corrected in the engine database and generation descriptions.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The six most common failure patterns across the M3 range — symptoms, costs and urgency.

problems.description: The defining E90 M3 issue — rod bearings fail catastrophically, scoring the crankshaft and destroying the engine. Cold-start knock or metallic noise at high RPM is the warning. Affects E90/E92/E93 M3 (2007–2013). £6,000–£10,000+ recon. Preventative replacement: £2,500–£4,000.

The defining F80 M3 issue — the crank hub is not keyed to the crankshaft and relies on friction alone. Under high load (especially on tuned cars), it can spin, causing piston-to-valve contact. Affects F80 M3 (2014–2020). £7,000–£10,000+ recon. Preventative fix: £1,500–£3,000.

The defining E46 M3 issues — VANOS pump failure and rod bearing wear. VANOS failure causes loss of power and rattling; rod bearing failure destroys the engine. Affects E46 M3 (2000–2006). £5,000–£8,000 recon. Preventative VANOS: £1,500–£3,000; rod bearings: £2,500–£4,000.
The E30 M3's S14 is durable but the timing chain tensioner and rod bearings are wear items. Rattling on cold start is the warning. Affects E30 M3 (1985–1991). Specialist-only work.

The E90 and E46 M3's cooling systems are common failure points — plastic water pump impellers, expansion tanks, and thermostat housings degrade. Overheating kills the alloy heads. Replace proactively every 60,000 miles on both generations.
The G80 M3's S58 is widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern performance engine. No major failure patterns — just standard maintenance. The G80 is the M3 to buy for worry-free ownership.

marketIntelligence.h2: BMW M3 Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us in 2025

signals.demandTrend: ⬆ Rising — the F80 is a modern classic [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
⬆ Rising — the V8 M3 is appreciating [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
⬆ Rising — new generation entering used market [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]

insights: Interesting insight: The M3 generates a different kind of enquiry than the rest of the BMW range — owners are enthusiasts doing due diligence on specific known issues (S65 rod bearings, S55 crank hub, S54 VANOS) rather than families facing unexpected failures. The M3 buyer knows what they're getting into — they just want to know which generation and which engine to choose. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)

The F80 M3 Competition is the sweet spot in the used market — 444 bhp, the S55 engine, and depreciated into sensible territory (£30,000–£40,000). The crank hub is the only caveat — budget for a hub fix (£1,500–£3,000) or buy one that's already been done. (Editorial synthesis.)

The E90 M3 with the S65 V8 is the appreciating classic — 8,400 rpm, 414 bhp, and the only V8 M3 ever made. Rod bearings are the defining risk — budget £2,500–£4,000 for preventative replacement or buy one that's already been done. The E46 M3 with the S54 is the driver's choice — the last naturally-aspirated straight-six M3 and arguably the greatest M car ever made. (Editorial synthesis.)

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW M3 Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one M3 based on this page, buy an F80 M3 Competition with the S55 — it's the sweet spot in the range: 444 bhp, proven engine, and depreciated into sensible territory (£30,000–£40,000). Just budget for a crank hub fix (£1,500–£3,000) or buy one that's already been done. If your budget stretches, the G80 with the S58 is the most reliable M3 ever made. And on an E90 S65, budget for rod bearings before you buy — it's £2,500–£4,000 well spent.

replacementCosts.h2: BMW M3 Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for used, reconditioned and rebuilt engines — supply only, with labour guidance.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a BMW M3 engine typically takes 14–22 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE]. The M3 rule — pay to play: A £7,000–£10,000+ S55/S58 recon against a £40,000 F80 sits at 18–25% — ✅ safe. Against a £60,000 G80, the same recon is 12–17% — ✅ safe. Against a £12,000 E90, a £6,000–£10,000+ S65 recon is 50–83% — ⚠️ watch. Against a £15,000 E46, a £5,000–£8,000 S54 recon is 33–53% — ⚠️ watch. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Factor in preventative work (S65 rod bearings: £2,500–£4,000; S55 crank hub: £1,500–£3,000; S54 VANOS/rod bearings: £2,500–£4,000) as part of the purchase cost — it's the smart money on any M3.

Against a £12,000 E90, a £6,000–£10,000+ S65 recon is 50–83% — ⚠️ watch. Against a £15,000 E46, a £5,000–£8,000 S54 recon is 33–53% — ⚠️ watch. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Factor in preventative work (S65 rod bearings: £2,500–£4,000; S55 crank hub: £1,500–£3,000; S54 VANOS/rod bearings: £2,500–£4,000) as part of the purchase cost — it's the smart money on any M3.

engineEvolution.h2: How the BMW M3 Engine Range Evolved — 1985 to Today

eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW's motorsport division created the M3 for Group A touring car racing. The S14 four-cylinder was derived from the M10 and M88 engines, producing 195 bhp in road trim.
eras.worthKnowing: The S14 is a masterpiece of 1980s engineering — revving to 7,500 rpm with individual throttle bodies. The E30 M3 is now a six-figure classic — values are only going one way.

eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW moved to a straight-six for the E36 M3 — the S50 was a development of the M50 engine with individual throttle bodies and a variable-length intake manifold.
eras.worthKnowing: The Euro-spec S50 (286–321 bhp) is the one to buy — the US-spec S52 is good but less special. Cooling systems and rear subframe mounts are the defining caveats.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The S54 is one of BMW's finest engines — 343 bhp, 8,000 rpm redline, individual throttle bodies, and a six-speed manual gearbox.
eras.worthKnowing: The S54 is a masterpiece but VANOS and rod bearings are the defining risks. A sorted E46 with these addressed is a six-figure classic.

eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW's only V8 M3 — a 4.0L naturally-aspirated V8 that revved to 8,400 rpm, derived from the S85 V10 in the E60 M5.
eras.worthKnowing: The S65 is a masterpiece but rod bearings are the defining risk. Preventative replacement at 60,000–80,000 miles is the smart money. The E90 saloon is the rarest; the E92 coupé is the most desirable.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The first turbocharged M3 — the S55 was a development of the N55 with closed-deck block, forged internals, and twin turbos.
eras.worthKnowing: The S55 is proven and powerful (425–444 bhp), but the crank hub is the Achilles heel. The Competition (444 bhp) is the sweet spot; the CS (454 bhp) is the ultimate F80.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The S58 is widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern performance engine — closed-deck block, forged internals, and a revised crank hub design.
eras.worthKnowing: The G80 is the most powerful M3 ever (473–503 bhp) and the most reliable. Available with a six-speed manual or automatic. The G80 is the M3 for the modern era — buy it with confidence.

whoShouldBuy.h2: Who Should Buy a BMW M3?

profiles.verdict: The M3 is built for this — 414–503 bhp, rear-wheel-drive, and one of the finest chassis in the world. The F80 Competition is the used sweet spot (£30,000–£40,000); the G80 is the future. Buy the newest S58 you can afford, or hunt for a well-maintained F80 Competition with the crank hub done.

The E46 M3 with the S54 is the greatest M3 ever made — a future classic that's already appreciating. The E90 M3 with the S65 is the only V8 M3 — a masterpiece that will be remembered as one of the greatest naturally-aspirated engines ever built. Both are worth proper investment.

The M3 is a daily-drivable performance car — the F80 and G80 are comfortable, practical, and have four doors (unlike the M4). The F80 is the better daily driver; the G80 is more refined. The E46 and E90 are weekend cars.

The M3 is one of the best track-day cars you can buy — the E90's V8 is a track-day hero; the F80's turbo torque is addictive; the G80 is the fastest. Budget for cooling upgrades and preventative maintenance.

A £15,000 E46 M3 is the entry point — but budget for VANOS (£1,500–£3,000) and rod bearings (£2,500–£4,000) before you buy. A £20,000 E90 M3 is the V8 entry point — but budget for rod bearings (£2,500–£4,000) before you buy. The F80 Competition at £30,000–£40,000 is the value sweet spot — 444 bhp, proven engine, and the crank hub is the only caveat.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My BMW M3 Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: Rattling on cold start from an S65? Crank hub spin on an S55? On the M3, the answer is almost always repair — the values are strong enough to justify engine work, especially on the E46, E90, F80 and G80. Get a personalised repair-vs-replace verdict using real UK cost data.
